title: Slow to Become Angry
reading:
The third instruction James gives is the one that ties everything together: slow to become angry. Not never angry — slow.

What it really means: anger doesn't lead your emotions. Feelings are real, but they don't get full control. Think about your actions before you express them.

In real life that looks like:
Feeling the anger rise — and acknowledging it without handing it the wheel.
Exercising self-control instead of reacting.
Asking for help when it feels like too much to manage alone.

The opposite — instant rage, self-harm, blaming, breaking things, shouting — is not strength. It is anger that has been handed authority it was never meant to have.

This is why James says what he does: human anger does not produce the righteousness that God desires. Righteousness is the fruit of the Spirit — love, joy, peace, patience, kindness, goodness, faithfulness, gentleness, and yes, self-control. Anger that has taken over produces the opposite of every one of those.

Colossians 3:8 puts it plainly: put off anger, wrath, malice, slander, and filthy language from your mouth. Not because anger itself is a sin, but because uncontrolled anger opens doors that are very hard to close.

James is not condemning you for feeling. He is inviting you into something better — a life where the Holy Spirit leads, not your worst moment.
reflection: Where does anger tend to take over in your life — at home, in relationships, at work? What would it look like to let the Holy Spirit lead in those moments instead?
abide: Father, help me be slow to anger. Not because I suppress what I feel, but because I trust You with it. Let the fruit of the Spirit flow through me.
